summary: Joel Spolsky argues that software should be designed for the least technically proficient users, because doing so makes the software easier for everyone. He highlights that users fundamentally don't like to read, and will ignore large blocks of text in user interfaces. The post serves as a teaser for Chapter 6 of his 'UI for Programmers' series, which covers the topic in depth.
key_points: Design for the least experienced users and everyone benefits from the improved usability
Users don't read — give them a lot of words and they will happily ignore them
UI text should be minimal because users skip over lengthy instructions
money_quotes: If you design your program for the, um, challenged users, everyone will find them easier to use.
Users don't like to read. Give them a lot of words, and they will happily ignore them.
key_insight: Designing for the least sophisticated users makes software easier for all users, particularly because people don't read UI text.
